profile.behavior: Walks and forages at the water’s edge, often bobbing the rear of its body; commonly feeds among wet leaves, moss, stones, and shallow running water.
field_marks: Plain olive-brown upperparts with no wingbars
White underparts with heavy dark streaking on breast and sides
Usually plain white throat and warm buff wash on flanks and undertail
Pinkish legs and a short, square-tipped to rounded tail
profile.habitat: Forested streams, creeks, brooks, ravines, and other shaded freshwater edges; favors flowing water and nearby wet ground.

sources.title: Louisiana Waterthrush Overview — All About Birds

findings: Identity is consistent with Louisiana Waterthrush: olive-brown upperparts, a bold white supercilium, mostly plain white throat, heavy dark breast-and-flank streaking, pink legs, and no wingbars match the supplied field photos and field-guide descriptions.

The bird is anatomically coherent: one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and a normal paired-wing body plan are depicted. The visible wing and separate wing study are plausible; feet/toes are somewhat oversized and sharply rendered but not malformed.

The main bird’s bill is drawn rather long and fine, and its tail reads slightly longer and more tapered than the compact, short square-tipped-to-rounded tail typically emphasized for this species. These are minor proportional departures, not an identification-breaking error.
Common name, scientific name (Parkesia motacilla), and family (Parulidae) are correct. Habitat and foraging/bobbing behavior are supported by both sources.

Length and weight match Audubon’s published range. The printed 20–25 cm (8–10 in) wingspan matches Audubon, but differs from All About Birds’ 24–27 cm (9.4–10.6 in) range; retain a source-specific citation or broaden the stated range to avoid an apparent cross-source discrepancy.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears on the plate.

verification_sources.title: Louisiana Waterthrush Identification — All About Birds
